text: The surgeon prepares for a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a bulky uterus, approximately fifteen centimeters in size. A supraumbilical port is placed, followed by a small stab incision for a two-millimeter entry. The abdominal wall is lifted to introduce the Veress needle, and irrigation, suction, and the hanging drop test are performed to confirm the needle's placement inside the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon attaches the tubing of the insufflator and begins CO₂ insufflation at a rate of one liter per minute. The insufflator's preset pressure is set to fifteen millimeters of mercury, and the actual pressure gradually increases from ten to fifteen. Once the pressure stabilizes, the surgeon removes the Veress needle and enlarges the incision to eleven millimeters before introducing the optical port.

The surgeon connects the gas and applies the baseball diamond concept for port placement, positioning the first arc at 18 cm from the uterine artery and the second at 24 cm with a telescope. Two trocars are placed 7.5 cm lateral to the midline for ipsilateral ports, and a contralateral port is created for the assistant. The uterine manipulator is introduced after dilating the cervix with a Hegar dilator, and the cervix is held with a Valsalva anterior leaf.
The surgeon introduces a uterine sound for further cervical dilatation, using a speculum to aid in the process.

The surgeon uses a number five Hegar dilator to prepare the cervix, followed by the insertion of a Mangeshkar uterine manipulator equipped with a 3.5 cm colpotomizer. Contralateral traction is applied, and the round ligament is coagulated. The uterus appears bulky with a right wall fibroid and multiple intramural fibroids.

The surgeon begins by coagulating the right round ligament, followed by the fallopian tube and ovarian ligament, using a ligature device. The right adnexa is separated. Contralateral traction is applied, and the left round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovarian ligament are similarly coagulated and dissected. The large uterus limits space, with the sigmoid colon visible below the left ovarian ligament.

The surgeon retroverts the uterus and opens the anterior leaf of the broad ligament using left-hand graspers to stretch the peritoneum. A harmonic device is used to create a clear areolar tissue plane for separation. The assistant rotates the uterus from the five o'clock to seven o'clock position, aligning it in the midline. The uterovesical fold is opened, and the procedure continues towards the right side.

The surgeon lifts the bladder and pushes the uterus towards the seven o'clock position. The uterine artery is visible as the surgeon walks over to the right side of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. The uterus is then repositioned to the six o'clock position, and the shiny vaginal fascia is exposed. Blunt dissection is used to separate the bladder, with lateralization performed towards the bladder pillar.

The surgeon opens the posterior peritoneum, carefully separating it up to the arc of the uterosacral ligament due to the presence of a large fibroid in the posterior wall. A grasper is used to lift the uterus, while a 30-degree telescope positioned at six o'clock provides a clear view of the posterior cul-de-sac for further dissection.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine artery on the right side without cutting, using a ligature device. The posterior peritoneum is separated, and the uterine artery is coagulated and cut at two places. The posterior peritoneum is further reflected to facilitate holding the uterus.
The surgeon divides the uterine artery on the right side, followed by the division of the uterine artery on the left side, completing the separation of these structures.

The surgeon begins the colpotomy using the tip of the harmonic scalpel, starting on the left side and extending anteriorly. The anterior colpotomy is carefully extended to the right side, ensuring gentle use of the harmonic to avoid damaging the colpotomizer. The right side colpotomy is completed by bringing the harmonic scalpel to the right port. The posterior colpotomy is performed in two halves, first from the right side and then from the left side.

The surgeon pulls the uterus into the vagina and bisects it for removal due to its large size. A #1 Vicryl suture is used to take bites on the utero-sacral ligament and posterior vaginal vault, ensuring the bladder is not pricked by pushing it posteriorly. Suturing continues from right to left without tying the knot initially, taking a total of six bites. The left corner is sutured by taking the utero-sacral ligament and anterior fornix, locking the suture. The assistant holds the suture tail while the surgeon continues with interlocking sutures to close the vaginal vault.

The surgeon completes the valve closure by interlocking the sutures and using a surgeon's knot to terminate the closure. The suture tail is pulled through a 5mm port, and the cannula is removed to extract the needle. Suction irrigation is performed to clear the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the surgical site for any signs of bleeding and confirms there is none. Fluid is aspirated from the posterior cul-de-sac to clear the surgical field.
